ELPAC Level 1
Lexile 650–1000L
DTR | Listen to a Speaker Support an Opinion
LSO-MS6-L1-6501000-MSELA6-MKT-LAT-01 | MS ELA: Grade 6 | Establish and Maintain Formal Style | Mock Trial | Latinx / Latin American Experiences
Choosing Every Word
Click 🎧 to listen.
Listen to a conversation between two students. The boy is discussing his opinion about speaking in a formal style. Listen carefully. You will hear the conversation only once. After listening, you will answer four questions about the conversation and the evidence the boy gives to support his opinion. As you listen, you may take notes.
Listen to the Conversation

Boy: Daniela, I have been thinking about our mock trial. I think we should talk in a formal way when we present our case, not the way we talk at lunch.

Girl: A formal way? But will that not sound stiff? I thought we just had to make our points clearly.

Boy: We do, but how we say it matters too. When my abuela visits, I do not talk to her like I talk to my friends. With her I use the respectful words my family saves for elders, not the casual ones for my cousins. My family taught me to change how you speak depending on who is listening.

Girl: Oh, I never thought about it like that. So you mean the courtroom is one of those places where you switch?

Boy: Exactly. A judge is listening, so it fits to use careful, respectful words. I noticed it at my tio's citizenship ceremony. Everyone there spoke so carefully and clearly. Nobody used slang. It made the whole thing feel important and real.

Girl: That makes sense. But what if the judge thinks we are trying too hard?

Boy: I do not think so. When people speak formally, listeners take them more seriously. Lawyers practice saying things in a clear, formal way so the jury trusts them. If we say I object instead of hey, that is not fair, the judge knows we understand the rules.

Girl: Okay, I am starting to agree. So we plan out our sentences ahead of time instead of just talking?

Boy: Right. We can still sound like ourselves. Formal does not mean fake. It means we choose our words on purpose, the way my family does with our elders. That respect is what wins people over.

Girl: I like that. Let us write out our opening the formal way and practice it tonight.

1QUESTION 1:
Why does the boy mention his abuela?
2QUESTION 2:
Where did the boy first learn to switch between a formal and a casual way of speaking?
3QUESTION 3:
What does the boy say he noticed at his tio's citizenship ceremony?
4QUESTION 4:
According to the boy, why does speaking in a formal way help in the mock trial?
Answer all 4 questions to submit.
Choose the best answer for each question. You may change your answers before submitting.
SMART SUPPORT:
Go back to the conversation. Listen for the moment the boy says why a formal way of talking fits the courtroom. Listen for where he learned to switch how he speaks. Listen for what he says about a time people spoke carefully.
CA ELD: PI.A.3, PI.B.5, PI.B.7, PI.B.8, PII.A.1 | CCSS: W.6.1.d, W.6.2.e | MS ELA: Grade 6
